title: The practice of being honest when someone asks how you are
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
The script we all know by heart

metaDescription:
The script we all know by heart 

 Someone asks how you are. It's a neighbor at the mailbox, a colleague in the breakroom, a relative at the dinner table....
htmlContent:
The script we all know by heart

Someone asks how you are. It's a neighbor at the mailbox, a colleague in the breakroom, a relative at the dinner table. And without thinking, you say it. "I'm fine." "I'm good." "I'm okay." Maybe you even add a little smile, a nod, a shrug that says everything's normal here.

I've done this thousands of times. I still do it, honestly. There's a part of me that wants to say something real, something true. But another part kicks in first. The part that learned early that honesty costs too much. That the real answer would make people uncomfortable. That they didn't actually want to know.

Here's the thing. The question "how are you" in our culture is not a question. It's a greeting. A ritual. A script we all follow. You say fine, I say fine, we both move on with our day. No harm, no mess, no risk. But if you're living with complex trauma, that script becomes a cage. You're not just saying words. You're practicing a kind of disappearance. You're learning, over and over, that your real experience doesn't belong in the room.

I want to talk about what happens when you break that script. When you decide to be honest, even a little, about how you actually are. Not because it's easy. Not because it always goes well. But because the practice of honesty is one of the most direct ways to reconnect with yourself after trauma. And that reconnection is the whole point of this work.

Let's start with what's at stake.

What you're protecting by saying "I'm fine"

When I say "I'm fine" and I'm not fine, I'm not lying to be polite. I'm protecting something. Maybe I'm protecting you from the weight of my story. Maybe I'm protecting myself from your pity, your judgment, or your well-meaning advice that misses the mark entirely. Maybe I'm protecting the fragile sense of safety I've built by keeping my pain invisible.

This isn't weakness. It's survival. And it's wired deep.

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and threat. When someone asks how you are, your body makes a split-second assessment. Is this person safe? Do they have capacity for my truth? Will my honesty put me at risk? And most of the time, the answer is no. So your ventral vagal system says play it safe, and you give the scripted answer.

Stay with me here. This isn't about blaming yourself for being dishonest. It's about recognizing that your nervous system is doing exactly what it evolved to do. It's keeping you alive in a world where honesty has historically gotten you hurt. The problem is that this same system, when it runs on autopilot, also keeps you isolated. It keeps your pain locked inside your body, unspoken, unprocessed, unseen.

I've had moments where I tried to be honest and it backfired. I told someone I was struggling and they changed the subject. I told someone I was scared and they told me to "just think positive." I told someone I was exhausted from the work of healing and they said "maybe you're trying too hard." Each time, a part of me learned the same old lesson: keep it to yourself.

But here's what I've also learned. The cost of staying silent is higher than the cost of a few awkward conversations. When you never tell anyone how you really are, you start to believe that how you really are is wrong. That your pain is too much. That your needs are unreasonable. That you are, at your core, unworthy of care.

That belief will kill you. Not all at once. Slowly. In the way that shame erodes your sense of self over years and decades. In the way that isolation makes your nervous system more dysregulated, not less. In the way that pretending to be fine keeps you from getting the help you actually need.

So what do we do? We start small. We practice. We take tiny risks with people who have earned some trust. And we learn to tolerate the discomfort of being seen.

The muscle of honest response

I think of honesty as a muscle. You don't walk into a gym and bench press 300 pounds on your first day. You start with the bar. You learn the movement. You build strength over time. Same thing here.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker, the author, talks about the "fawn response" - the survival strategy where you prioritize other people's comfort over your own truth. Sound familiar? It's the engine behind that automatic "I'm fine." Walker's work gives you language for what's happening and a path toward something different.

The first step is just noticing. Next time someone asks how you are, pause for one second before you answer. Notice what your body does. Does your throat tighten? Does your chest get heavy? Do you feel a pull toward the script? Just notice. Don't change anything yet. Just observe.

Then, when you're ready, try a small edit. Instead of "I'm fine," try "I'm hanging in there." Instead of "I'm good," try "I've been better, but I'm okay." Instead of "Everything's great," try "It's been a rough week, honestly." These aren't full disclosures. They're cracks in the armor. Small openings that let a little light in.

Notice what just happened in your body. If you felt a flutter of anxiety reading those alternatives, that's normal. Your nervous system is saying danger, we're breaking the rules. But here's the thing. Breaking the rules, when done with intention and with relatively safe people, is how you teach your system that honesty is not always dangerous. That you can be seen and still be safe.

Janina Fisher, a trauma therapist and expert in structural dissociation, talks about how trauma survivors often have parts of self that hold different responses. There's the part that wants to be honest, the part that's terrified of honesty, the part that's angry at having to perform wellness, the part that just wants to be left alone. All of these parts are valid. None of them are wrong. The practice is about letting them all have a voice, not just the one that says "I'm fine."

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It's a framework for understanding the different voices inside you without pathologizing any of them. When you can say "a part of me wants to be honest, and a part of me is terrified," you're already more honest than "I'm fine."

When honesty doesn't land

I need to be real with you about something. Not everyone is ready for your honesty. Some people will react badly. Some will shut down. Some will try to fix you. Some will tell you you're being too negative or too dramatic or too much. This hurts. It can feel like proof that you were right to stay silent all along.

But here's what I've learned. Other people's reactions are not a measure of your worth or the validity of your experience. They're a measure of their own capacity. Their own comfort with discomfort. Their own unresolved stuff.

When I started being more honest, I lost some relationships. Not many, but some. People who couldn't handle the real me. People who needed me to stay small and manageable. And losing those relationships hurt. But it also cleared space. Space for people who could actually sit with me in my pain. People who didn't need me to be fine so they could feel fine.

Think about it. If you're always fine, nobody knows what you need. Nobody can support you. Nobody can show up for you because they don't know you need . The irony is that the armor you built to protect yourself also keeps out the connection you're starving for.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. Because here's the thing. Honesty without boundaries isn't honesty. It's dumping. And dumping doesn't help anyone. The practice is learning to say "I'm not okay, and I don't need you to fix it, I just needed to say it out loud." Or "I'm struggling, and I could use a hug but not advice right now." Boundaries make honesty sustainable.

I'm not saying you need to tell everyone everything. That's not the goal. The goal is to have at least one or two people in your life who know the real answer when they ask how you are. People who have earned that trust. People who can hold your pain without collapsing or trying to erase it.

If you don't have those people yet, that's okay. You can start with yourself. Ask yourself how you are, and wait for the honest answer. Write it down. Say it out loud in an empty room. Let your own system know that you're ready to hear the truth, even if nobody else is.

The return to yourself

This article is in the "the-return" category on this site. And that's exactly what this practice is. A return. A return to the parts of yourself you learned to hide. A return to your own voice, your own experience, your own truth. A return to the possibility of being known.

Every time you answer honestly, even a little, you're sending a signal to your nervous system. We're safe enough to be real. We're safe enough to be seen. We're safe enough to exist as we actually are. That signal, repeated over time, literally rewires your brain. It builds new neural pathways. It strengthens the ventral vagal system. It creates more capacity for connection.

This is not about becoming a person who overshares with strangers or unloads trauma at the grocery store. This is about reclaiming the right to have an inner experience that matters. The right to not be fine. The right to be complicated, messy, contradictory, and still worthy of love.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. It's about learning to say yes to what's true, even when what's true is painful. Even when what's true is "I'm not okay." Radical acceptance doesn't mean resignation. It means you stop fighting reality and start meeting it with compassion.

I still struggle with this. I still say "I'm fine" when I'm not. I still have parts that want to hide. But I also have more moments now where I take a breath, feel the fear, and say something real. And in those moments, I feel something shift. A loosening. A softening. A sense that I'm coming home to myself.

That's the return. It's not a destination. It's a practice. A daily, imperfect, sometimes awkward practice of being honest when someone asks how you are. And letting yourself be answered.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - Pete Walker's guide to understanding the fawn response and recovering from C-PTSD
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's introduction to Internal Family Systems and parts work
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - Nedra Glover Tawwab's practical guide to setting boundaries with compassion
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
